1. **Set up the Cauchy integral representation.** Choose an open set V with K ⊆ V ⊆ V̄ ⊆ U on which f is holomorphic. By a standard "cycle around K in U\K" construction (sometimes called the Cauchy–Dixon or grid lemma), produce a finite collection of oriented line segments γ₁,…,γₙ contained in U \ K such that for every z ∈ K,
   f(z) = (1/2πi) Σⱼ ∫_{γⱼ} f(w)/(w − z) dw.
   The standard way: tile ℂ by a fine square grid, take the squares meeting K, and let the cycle be the boundary of their union, oriented so each square has winding number 1 around its interior points. Mathlib has the Cauchy integral formula for discs/circles but may not have this "grid cycle" version; the student likely has to build it as a helper lemma.

2. **Reduce to approximating Cauchy kernels.** Each integral ∫_{γⱼ} f(w)/(w−z) dw is a uniform limit on K of Riemann sums of the form Σₖ cₖ/(wₖ − z), with wₖ ∈ U \ K. So it suffices to approximate, uniformly on K, every function of the form z ↦ 1/(w − z) with w ∉ K by polynomials in z. (Standard ε/3-style argument: bound the Riemann-sum error using compactness of K and the fact that w stays a positive distance from K.)

3. **Pole-pushing lemma.** Show that the set
   S := { w ∈ ℂ \ K : z ↦ 1/(w − z) is uniformly approximable on K by polynomials }
   is both open and closed in ℂ \ K. Openness: if w₀ ∈ S and w is close to w₀, expand 1/(w−z) as a geometric series in (w − w₀)/(w₀ − z), which converges uniformly on K and reduces approximation of 1/(w−z) to approximation of powers of 1/(w₀−z), already in the algebra generated by S. Closedness uses a similar geometric estimate.

4. **Use connectedness of Kᶜ.** Since Kᶜ = ℂ \ K is connected (by hypothesis) and S is nonempty, open, and closed in Kᶜ, we get S = Kᶜ — provided we exhibit at least one w ∈ S. For |w| sufficiently large, 1/(w−z) = (1/w) Σₙ (z/w)ⁿ converges uniformly on K, giving polynomial approximations directly. So such a w exists, hence S = ℂ \ K.

5. **Assemble.** Combining steps 2 and 4: every Cauchy kernel z ↦ 1/(wₖ − z) with wₖ ∈ U\K ⊆ Kᶜ is polynomially approximable on K. Hence the Riemann sums, and thus f, are uniform limits on K of polynomials. Given ε > 0, chain three ε/3 estimates (cycle approximation, Riemann sum approximation, polynomial approximation of each kernel) to extract a single polynomial p with sup_K |p − f| < ε.

**Difficulty note.** This is a genuinely multi-page classical proof. Mathlib (as of writing) does not contain Runge's theorem, the grid-cycle version of Cauchy's formula, or the pole-pushing connectedness argument; each of steps 1, 3, 4 will likely need to be developed as standalone helper lemmas. Expect the formalization to be substantial (hundreds to a few thousand lines).
